metadata.disclaimer: Educational estimates only. Not tax, legal, or immigration advice. Verify with qualified professionals before relocating. Rules change frequently.

Portugal.tax_summary: NHR regime closed to new applicants in 2024. Foreign pensions now taxed at standard progressive rates up to 48%. US-Portugal tax treaty helps reduce double taxation. Foreign Tax Credit usually offsets most US tax liability.
Portugal.key_exemptions: NHR 2.0 (IFICI) replaced NHR but excludes pension income. Existing NHR holders grandfathered through their 10-year period. US-Portugal tax treaty provides some relief.
Portugal.visa_summary: D7 visa for passive income earners including retirees. Leads to permanent residency after 5 years, citizenship after 5 years. Requires proof of passive income and private health insurance.
Portugal.healthcare_notes: Public SNS healthcare accessible after residency validated. Private insurance required for visa application. High quality public system once enrolled.
Portugal.tax_rate_why: Portugal's NHR tax break closed for new applicants in 2024, so foreign pensions now face normal progressive rates. The US–Portugal treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually prevent double taxation.
Portugal.tax_summary_why: You file in Portugal on local income, but your US return doesn't go away — the treaty is your safety net, not a free pass.
Portugal.key_exemptions_why: Locked in NHR before 2024? You're grandfathered for the rest of your term. New arrivals get IFICI or standard rates — neither treats pension income like old NHR.
Portugal.healthcare_notes_why: You'll need private insurance to get the visa, then public SNS opens once you're properly registered.
Portugal.visa_summary_why: The D7 is one of Europe's more reachable retirement visas if your income is mostly passive.

Spain.tax_summary: No special tax regime for pension income. Foreign pensions taxed at standard progressive rates. Spain's Golden Visa closed April 2025. Beckham Law excludes passive income. US-Spain tax treaty in place.
Spain.key_exemptions: Non-Lucrative Visa requires ~€28,800/year passive income. No flat-rate pension exemption available. Foreign Tax Credit offsets most US tax owed.
Spain.visa_summary: Non-Lucrative Visa for retirees with passive income. Golden Visa closed April 2025. Leads to permanent residency after 5 years. Annual renewal required initially.
Spain.healthcare_notes: Excellent public healthcare once registered as resident. Private insurance required for initial visa application.
Spain.tax_rate_why: There's no special pension deal here — your foreign pension faces normal Spanish progressive rates. Budget for that, and lean on the US–Spain treaty and Foreign Tax Credit to avoid double taxation.
Spain.tax_summary_why: The Golden Visa is gone, so the Non-Lucrative Visa is the main retiree path — passive income proof and annual renewals are part of the real cost of settling in.
Spain.key_exemptions_why: No flat-rate pension exemption means your visa income threshold (~€28,800/year) is the gate — plan for that before you pick a neighborhood.
Spain.healthcare_notes_why: You'll need private insurance to apply, then Spain's public system becomes excellent once you're registered as a resident.
Spain.visa_summary_why: Five years to permanent residency is a clear runway if you want EU roots without a work visa.

Italy.tax_summary: Italy's Article 24-ter allows qualifying foreign pensioners to pay a flat 7% tax on all foreign income for up to 10 years. Must relocate to specific southern Italian municipalities. US-Italy tax treaty in place.
Italy.key_exemptions: 7% flat tax on foreign pension income for up to 10 years. Must move to qualifying southern Italian town with population under 20,000. Significant savings vs standard progressive rates up to 43%.
Italy.visa_summary: Elective Residency Visa for retirees with passive income. Requires proof of stable passive income and private health insurance. Leads to permanent residency after 5 years.
Italy.healthcare_notes: Excellent public healthcare (SSN) accessible after registering as resident. Private insurance required for initial visa.
Italy.top_reason: 7% flat tax on foreign income for 10 years
Italy.tax_rate_why: The 7% flat regime is real savings — but only in qualifying southern towns under Article 24-ter, not anywhere in Italy you fancy.
Italy.tax_summary_why: You trade location flexibility for a decade of predictable tax on foreign income — worth it if a small southern comune fits your lifestyle.
Italy.key_exemptions_why: Standard rates up to 43% apply outside the special regime — confirm your municipality qualifies before you sign a lease.
Italy.healthcare_notes_why: Private insurance gets you through the visa phase; SSN public care is excellent once you're registered.
Italy.visa_summary_why: Elective Residency works for passive-income retirees — bureaucracy is the main friction, not the income threshold.

Greece.tax_summary: Article 5B allows qualifying foreign pensioners to elect a 7% flat tax on all foreign source income for up to 15 years. Must come from a country with tax cooperation agreement with Greece. US qualifies.
Greece.key_exemptions: 7% flat tax on all foreign income for up to 15 years. Requires minimum €250,000 real estate investment for Golden Visa OR €3,500/month income for retirement visa. Excellent tax deal for US retirees.
Greece.visa_summary: Financially Independent Person Visa requires proof of passive income, health insurance, and clean criminal record. Golden Visa (real estate investment) also available. EU Schengen access.
Greece.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare accessible after residency. Private insurance required for visa. High quality private hospitals in Athens and major cities.
Greece.top_reason: 7% flat tax on foreign income for 15 years
Greece.tax_rate_why: Article 5B's 7% flat rate on foreign income for up to 15 years is among Europe's best deals — if you meet the investment and relocation rules.
Greece.tax_summary_why: The flat election is optional but powerful — compare it against standard progressive rates before you declare residency.
Greece.key_exemptions_why: The €250,000 property tie-in is non-negotiable for the special regime — factor that into your upfront capital plan.
Greece.healthcare_notes_why: Public care opens after residency; private insurance is required to get your visa in the first place.
Greece.visa_summary_why: The Financially Independent Person route suits retirees with steady passive income who want island or mainland Mediterranean life.

France.tax_summary: Once resident in France, distributions from US retirement accounts (401k, IRA) are exempt from French local taxes. France preserves Roth account tax-exempt status. Progressive income tax otherwise up to 45%. US-France tax treaty in place.
France.key_exemptions: US pension and Social Security income exempt from French tax under US-France treaty. Roth distributions maintain tax-exempt status. Foreign Tax Credit available for any remaining liability.
France.visa_summary: Long Stay Visitor Visa for retirees with passive income. Must prove sufficient income to support yourself. Leads to residency card renewal annually, then 10-year card.
France.healthcare_notes: World-class public healthcare (Securite Sociale) accessible after 3 months legal residency. Private insurance recommended initially.
France.top_reason: US pension and 401k distributions exempt from local tax
France.tax_rate_why: The US–France treaty can exempt US retirement account distributions from French tax once you're resident — that's a meaningful break if your income is mostly 401(k) or IRA draws.
France.tax_summary_why: French tax is complex, but pension and Social Security treatment under the treaty often surprises retirees in a good way — get it mapped professionally.
France.key_exemptions_why: Roth and investment income follow different rules than traditional pensions — don't assume one exemption covers everything.
France.healthcare_notes_why: Sécurité Sociale is world-class once you're in the system — plan for private cover during the first months of legal residence.
France.visa_summary_why: The Long Stay Visitor visa is the classic retiree path — prove sufficient passive income and you can live in France year-round.

Croatia.tax_summary: Progressive income tax with rates of 20% up to HRK 360,000 and 30% above. EU member since 2013, Eurozone since 2023. No special retirement tax regime. US-Croatia tax treaty in place.
Croatia.key_exemptions: No special pension exemption. Foreign Tax Credit offsets US tax liability. Relatively low tax rates compared to Western Europe.
Croatia.visa_summary: Third-country nationals (including US) can apply for temporary residence with proof of passive income. EU membership means strong rule of law and stability.
Croatia.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare available after residency registration. Quality is good in cities, limited in rural areas. Private insurance recommended.
Croatia.tax_rate_why: Progressive rates apply to your income here — The US tax treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually keep you from paying twice on the same income.
Croatia.tax_summary_why: Your specific income mix (pension, investments, part-time work) determines the practical bill — the summary above is the starting point, not your personal rate.
Croatia.key_exemptions_why: Foreign Tax Credit on your US return is usually the backstop — local exemptions define what you owe here first.
Croatia.healthcare_notes_why: You'll likely need private insurance to get the visa and for day-to-day care — Medicare doesn't cover you abroad.
Croatia.visa_summary_why: A formal retirement or passive-income visa path exists — income proof and insurance are usually the main hurdles.

Hungary.tax_summary: Hungary has the lowest flat personal income tax in the EU at 15%. Applies to almost all income types including pensions. Simple and predictable for retirement planning. US-Hungary tax treaty in place.
Hungary.key_exemptions: 15% flat tax on all income including foreign pensions. Simple tax system with no complex brackets. Foreign Tax Credit generally covers US tax obligations.
Hungary.visa_summary: Hungary introduced new Guest Investor Program and White Card for remote workers. Temporary residence available for passive income earners. Budapest is a popular expat hub.
Hungary.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare available to residents. Quality varies. Private insurance strongly recommended for expats.
Hungary.tax_rate_why: The flat rate on the label is predictable for budgeting — The US tax treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually keep you from paying twice on the same income.
Hungary.tax_summary_why: Your specific income mix (pension, investments, part-time work) determines the practical bill — the summary above is the starting point, not your personal rate.
Hungary.key_exemptions_why: Foreign Tax Credit on your US return is usually the backstop — local exemptions define what you owe here first.
Hungary.healthcare_notes_why: Healthcare quality varies by city — private insurance is still the safe default for US retirees overseas.
Hungary.visa_summary_why: No dedicated retirement visa — explore passive-income, investor, or long-stay routes that match your situation.

Czech Republic.tax_summary: 15% flat tax on income up to 48x average wage (~$65,000), 23% above. Relatively low tax burden. EU member with stable economy. US-Czech Republic tax treaty in place.
Czech Republic.key_exemptions: Low flat tax rate. Foreign Tax Credit reduces US liability. No special retirement visa but long-term residence available for passive income earners.
Czech Republic.visa_summary: Long-term residence permit available for those with proof of passive income. Prague is a top expat destination. EU Schengen access.
Czech Republic.healthcare_notes: High quality public healthcare for registered residents. Private insurance recommended initially.
Czech Republic.top_reason: Low flat tax, affordable EU, Prague lifestyle
Czech Republic.tax_rate_why: The flat rate on the label is predictable for budgeting — The US tax treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually keep you from paying twice on the same income.

Poland.key_exemptions: Lower bracket 12% applies to most average retirement incomes. Foreign Tax Credit available. No special exemptions for foreign pensions.
Poland.visa_summary: Temporary residence for passive income earners. EU membership provides stability and Schengen travel. Poland is increasingly popular with expats.
Poland.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare available to registered residents. Quality improving but variable. Private insurance recommended.
Poland.top_reason: Very affordable EU country, rich history
Poland.tax_rate_why: Progressive rates apply to your income here — The US tax treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually keep you from paying twice on the same income.

Romania.tax_summary: Romania has one of the lowest flat income tax rates in the EU at 10%. No special retirement regime but very low base rate. EU and Schengen member as of 2025. US-Romania tax treaty in place.
Romania.key_exemptions: 10% flat tax on all income including pensions. Very favorable for US retirees as Foreign Tax Credit easily offsets US obligations. Bucharest emerging as expat hub.
Romania.visa_summary: Residence permit available for passive income earners. Low income requirements. EU and Schengen access as of 2025.
Romania.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare available but quality is variable. Private insurance strongly recommended. Good private hospitals in Bucharest and major cities.
Romania.top_reason: 10% flat tax, very affordable EU country
Romania.tax_rate_why: The flat rate on the label is predictable for budgeting — The US tax treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually keep you from paying twice on the same income.

Bulgaria.key_exemptions: 10% flat tax on worldwide income for residents. Foreign Tax Credit easily covers US obligations. No complex brackets or phase-outs.
Bulgaria.visa_summary: Long-term residence available for passive income earners. Low income threshold. EU Schengen access. Black Sea coast and mountain regions popular.
Bulgaria.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare available but quality is inconsistent. Private insurance strongly recommended. Good private clinics in Sofia.
Bulgaria.top_reason: 10% flat tax, lowest cost EU country

Serbia.tax_summary: Serbia has a 15% flat personal income tax. Not EU member but candidate status. Low cost of living. Belgrade is a growing expat hub. No US-Serbia tax treaty.
Serbia.key_exemptions: 15% flat tax rate. No formal retirement visa but long-term residence available. Foreign Tax Credit available for US obligations.
Serbia.visa_summary: Temporary residence available for passive income earners. No formal retirement visa program. Low barriers to entry. EU candidate country.
Serbia.healthcare_notes: Public healthcare available but limited for foreigners. Private insurance recommended. Good private clinics in Belgrade.
Serbia.top_reason: Very affordable, growing expat scene in Belgrade
Serbia.tax_rate_why: The flat rate on the label is predictable for budgeting — There's no US tax treaty — your US filing continues as usual, and local rules determine what gets taxed here.

Georgia.tax_summary: Georgia uses a territorial tax system. Foreign-sourced income including US pensions and Social Security is not taxed locally. Only Georgian-source income is taxable. Very favorable for US retirees.
Georgia.key_exemptions: Foreign income including US pensions, Social Security, and 401k distributions not taxed. Only local Georgian income taxed. Virtual Zone status available for digital workers.
Georgia.visa_summary: US citizens can stay in Georgia visa-free for 365 days per year. No formal retirement visa needed. Residence permit available for those who want to stay longer.
Georgia.healthcare_notes: Private healthcare is affordable and improving. International-standard hospitals in Tbilisi. Private insurance recommended. Medicare not accepted.
Georgia.top_reason: Foreign income not taxed, 365-day visa-free stay
Georgia.tax_rate_why: Georgia's territorial system means foreign pensions and Social Security usually stay off the local return — plus US citizens get a year visa-free.
Georgia.tax_summary_why: Only Georgian-source income is taxed — most retirees living on US income keep the full territorial benefit without a special visa.
Georgia.key_exemptions_why: The 365-day visa-free stay is unusual — many retirees cycle residency without a formal retirement program at all.
Georgia.healthcare_notes_why: Private care in Tbilisi is affordable and improving — insurance is cheap compared to US plans.
Georgia.visa_summary_why: No formal retirement visa needed for many Americans — the low cost of living and territorial tax are the main draws.

Morocco.tax_summary: Foreign pensions transferred to Morocco through official banking channels may receive favorable tax treatment. Progressive tax rates 0-38% but expat retirees often qualify for reduced rates on transferred pension income.
Morocco.key_exemptions: Pension income transferred through official channels taxed at reduced rates. 80% exemption on pension income transferred to Morocco in some cases. US-Morocco tax treaty in place.
Morocco.visa_summary: Residence permit available for retirees. Low income requirements. Warm climate, proximity to Europe. Marrakech and Essaouira popular with expats.
Morocco.healthcare_notes: Private healthcare recommended. International-standard clinics in Casablanca and Marrakech. Private insurance essential. Medicare not accepted.
Morocco.top_reason: Affordable, warm climate, close to Europe
Morocco.tax_rate_why: Progressive rates apply to your income here — The US tax treaty and Foreign Tax Credit usually keep you from paying twice on the same income.
Morocco.tax_summary_why: Your specific income mix (pension, investments, part-time work) determines the practical bill — the summary above is the starting point, not your personal rate.
Morocco.key_exemptions_why: The exemptions above are what separate a favorable setup from a costly one — verify they apply to your income sources.
Morocco.healthcare_notes_why: Healthcare quality varies by city — private insurance is still the safe default for US retirees overseas.
Morocco.visa_summary_why: No dedicated retirement visa — explore passive-income, investor, or long-stay routes that match your situation.

Tunisia.healthcare_notes: Private healthcare recommended. Good private clinics in Tunis and coastal cities. Private insurance essential.

Turkey.tax_summary: Progressive income tax 15-40%. No special retirement visa but long-term residence available. Istanbul popular with expats. High inflation environment in recent years.
Turkey.key_exemptions: No special pension exemptions. Foreign Tax Credit available. Dollar/euro purchasing power strong due to lira depreciation.
Turkey.visa_summary: Short-term residence permit renewable annually. Low income requirements. Istanbul and coastal areas (Bodrum, Antalya) very popular with expats.
Turkey.healthcare_notes: Good private healthcare in major cities. International hospitals in Istanbul. Private insurance strongly recommended.
Turkey.top_reason: Affordable, Istanbul lifestyle, strong dollar value
Turkey.tax_rate_why: Progressive rates apply to your income here — There's no US tax treaty — your US filing continues as usual, and local rules determine what gets taxed here.
Turkey.tax_summary_why: Your specific income mix (pension, investments, part-time work) determines the practical bill — the summary above is the starting point, not your personal rate.

Mexico.tax_rate_label: Non-resident: no local tax on foreign income
Mexico.tax_summary: If income is predominantly from US sources and you maintain a home outside Mexico, you may qualify as a non-resident, meaning US pension income is not taxed in Mexico. Full tax residency (183+ days) subjects worldwide income to Mexican taxes up to 35%.
Mexico.key_exemptions: Non-resident status means US pension income not taxed locally. Stay under 183 days or maintain non-resident status to preserve this. US-Mexico tax treaty in place.
Mexico.visa_summary: Temporary Resident Visa requires ~$2,700/month income or $108,000 in savings. Leads to permanent residency after 4 years. Large expat communities in San Miguel, Puerto Vallarta, Merida.
Mexico.healthcare_notes: Excellent private healthcare at fraction of US cost. IMSS public system available. Private insurance recommended. Many US expats use Mexican private care.
Mexico.top_reason: Close to US, large expat communities, no local tax on foreign income
Mexico.tax_rate_why: Keep non-resident status and your US pension often stays untaxed locally — a big draw for Americans here. Cross 183 days and Mexico can tax worldwide income up to 35%.
Mexico.tax_summary_why: Where you spend your days and whether Mexico considers you resident will change your bill more than the headline rate.
Mexico.key_exemptions_why: The US–Mexico treaty coordinates the rules, but residency status is the lever that matters day to day.
Mexico.healthcare_notes_why: Many retirees come specifically for care quality vs. price — budget private insurance until you're comfortable with IMSS.
Mexico.visa_summary_why: Income and savings paths are well-trodden — San Miguel, Puerto Vallarta, and Mérida have deep expat support.

Colombia.tax_summary: Non-residents taxed only on Colombian-source income. Full tax residency (183+ days in any 365 day period) subjects worldwide income to Colombian taxes up to 39%. Many expats carefully manage days to maintain non-resident status.
Colombia.key_exemptions: Stay under 183 days per 365-day period to maintain non-resident status and avoid Colombian tax on foreign income. Many expats hop to neighboring countries. No US-Colombia tax treaty.
Colombia.visa_summary: Low income requirement for retirement visa. Medellin and Cartagena are top destinations. Beautiful country with spring-like climate year-round in Medellin.
Colombia.healthcare_notes: Excellent and affordable private healthcare. Medellin has world-class medical facilities. Private insurance recommended. Medicare not accepted.
Colombia.top_reason: Low cost, great climate, excellent private healthcare
Colombia.tax_rate_why: Stay under 183 days in any 365-day window and Colombia generally taxes only local income — cross that line and worldwide income can hit progressive rates up to 39%.
Colombia.tax_summary_why: Many expats deliberately manage their calendar because residency timing is the whole game — there's no US–Colombia treaty to fall back on.
Colombia.key_exemptions_why: Without a treaty, your US filing continues as usual — local non-resident status is what keeps foreign pensions off the Colombian return.
Colombia.healthcare_notes_why: Medellín and Bogotá offer excellent private care at a fraction of US prices — budget insurance from day one since Medicare won't travel with you.
Colombia.visa_summary_why: At roughly $750/month, the pensioner visa is one of the most reachable in Latin America if Medellín's spring climate fits your plan.
Colombia.panel_heads_up: As a US citizen, you still file with the IRS on worldwide income. Colombia has no US tax treaty — your 183-day count determines whether local tax applies to foreign income. Map both sides with a cross-border advisor before you commit.

Panama.tax_summary: Panama uses a territorial tax system. US pensions, Social Security, and 401k distributions are not taxed locally. Only Panama-source income is taxable. Uses US dollar. No currency risk.
Panama.key_exemptions: All foreign-source income including US pensions, Social Security, and investment income is completely exempt from Panamanian tax. Dollar economy eliminates currency risk.
Panama.visa_summary: Pensionado visa grants immediate permanent residency. Extensive retiree discounts: 25% restaurants, 15-20% medical, 50% entertainment. Citizenship after 5 years. Dollar economy.
Panama.healthcare_notes: Good private healthcare in Panama City. JCI-accredited hospitals. International private insurance recommended. Medicare generally not accepted.
Panama.top_reason: Foreign income not taxed, dollar economy, Pensionado discounts
Panama.tax_rate_why: Territorial taxation means US pensions and Social Security typically stay off the Panamanian return — one of the clearest tax wins in the Americas.
Panama.tax_summary_why: Only Panama-source income is taxed locally, so most retirees living on US retirement income keep the full territorial benefit.
Panama.key_exemptions_why: No US–Panama treaty, but territorial rules often make that irrelevant for pension-only income — still confirm your specific sources.
Panama.healthcare_notes_why: Panama City has JCI-accredited hospitals at reasonable cost — insurance is essential since Medicare doesn't apply abroad.
Panama.visa_summary_why: The Pensionado visa grants immediate permanent residency plus retiree discounts — one of the smoothest paths in Central America.

Costa Rica.tax_summary: Costa Rica taxes only locally-sourced income. US pensions, Social Security, and foreign investment income are not taxed. Very retiree-friendly tax environment.
Costa Rica.key_exemptions: Foreign income including US pensions and Social Security completely exempt from Costa Rican tax. Only local income taxable. Foreign Tax Credit handles US obligations.
Costa Rica.visa_summary: Pensionado visa requires $1,000/month lifetime pension income. Leads to permanent residency after 3 years. Pura Vida lifestyle, biodiversity, safety, stable democracy.
Costa Rica.healthcare_notes: Excellent CAJA public healthcare system accessible after residency. Private insurance recommended initially. World-class healthcare at affordable prices.
Costa Rica.top_reason: Foreign income not taxed, excellent healthcare, safety
Costa Rica.tax_rate_why: Costa Rica taxes only local-source income, so US pensions and Social Security usually stay completely off the CR return.
Costa Rica.tax_summary_why: Territorial rules are straightforward for typical retirees — your main job is proving pension income for the visa, not fighting local tax on foreign income.
Costa Rica.key_exemptions_why: Foreign investment and pension income are exempt — employment or Costa Rican business income is what triggers local tax.
Costa Rica.healthcare_notes_why: CAJA public healthcare after residency is excellent value; many expats use private care for speed and keep CAJA as backup.
Costa Rica.visa_summary_why: $1,000/month lifetime pension qualifies you for Pensionado — permanent residency in three years if you stay the course.

Ecuador.tax_summary: Ecuador offers tax exemptions on certain foreign pension income. Dollar economy. Progressive tax rates but many exemptions available for retirees. Low cost of living.
Ecuador.key_exemptions: Senior discounts on many services. Some tax exemptions on foreign pension income. Dollar economy eliminates currency risk. Check current rules with tax professional.
Ecuador.visa_summary: Pensioner visa requires proof of pension income. Cuenca is the top expat city. Dollar economy. Very affordable cost of living.
Ecuador.healthcare_notes: Good private healthcare in Quito and Cuenca at fraction of US cost. IESS public system available. Private insurance recommended.
Ecuador.top_reason: Dollar economy, very affordable, Cuenca expat community
Ecuador.tax_rate_why: Territorial rules and senior discounts can leave foreign pension income lightly taxed or exempt — the dollar economy removes currency risk from your budget.
